petitioner_background: The petitioner is a journalist and author of poetry and literature who sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under INA section 203(b)(1)(A) (EB-1A). The Nebraska Service Center Director denied the petition, finding the petitioner had not demonstrated a one-time achievement or met at least three of the ten initial evidentiary criteria. The AAO summarily dismissed his subsequent appeal because it failed to specifically identify any erroneous conclusion of law or fact in the Director's decision, and no supplemental brief was submitted within the promised 30-day window.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the combined motion to reopen and motion to reconsider. The motion to reopen was dismissed because, while the petitioner submitted a FedEx mailing label and delivery confirmation purporting to show timely delivery of a brief to the AAO on May 23, 2019, he did not provide a copy of the actual brief or evidence he claimed to have mailed, and the mailing label did not identify him as the sender. The motion to reconsider was dismissed because the petitioner failed to show that the summary dismissal was incorrect based on the evidence of record at the time of the initial decision — the record did not and still does not include any supplement to the appeal. The AAO declined to consider the newly submitted motion brief discussing the merits of the case absent evidence that the appeal was summarily dismissed in error.
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural dismissal: The petitioner's original appeal (filed April 25, 2019 via Form I-290B) failed to include a statement specifically identifying any erroneous conclusion of law or fact as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v), and no supplemental brief was submitted within the promised 30 days, resulting in summary dismissal. On motion, the petitioner claimed to have mailed a 'brief/affidavit and additional evidence' via FedEx overnight on May 22, 2019, but (1) did not submit a copy of the purportedly mailed brief, and (2) the FedEx mailing label did not identify the petitioner as the sender, nor did he provide a receipt showing he paid the named shipper a fee — leaving no reliable link between the petitioner and the delivered package.
